text: Hello everyone, this is Dr. Lovett. I thought I'd put together a quick five-minute video of the robotic cholecystectomy.
I get a lot of questions from patients asking exactly what it is that we're doing and how the anatomy works, so I thought there's no better way than a video.
The surgeon places four robotic ports, each approximately eight millimeters in diameter, spanning from the left upper to the right lower part of the abdominal cavity.
The robotic surgical instruments, including graspers, are actively engaged in the procedure, manipulating tissues within the abdominal cavity.
The video displays a freeze frame highlighting the gallbladder, marked in green, within the surgical field.
The video shows small yellow adhesions from previous inflammatory episodes. The liver is clearly delineated, and the small bowel, specifically the duodenum, is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a cautery hook to lyse adhesions, cutting through the fibrous tissue. The camera then shifts to reveal additional anatomical structures for further examination.

The surgeon highlights the common bile duct in green, which runs between the liver and the small intestine, emphasizing the need to avoid injury to this structure. The yellow marks the Calot's lymph node, indicating the area for dissection.
The surgeon begins the dissection by opening the outermost layer of the peritoneum overlying the gallbladder using a cautery hook, carefully sliding the hook in and lifting the tissue away.
The robotic platform greatly facilitates this, allowing me to use my hand almost like a wrist as opposed to traditional laparoscopic instruments, which are very straight.

Now this video is sped up to two and a half time normal speed, so we don't really work quite this fast, but this patient did have quite favorable anatomy and a generally straightforward dissection. Gallbladders can range from quite straightforward, such as this one, to incredibly complex with lots of scarring and very difficult to identify anatomy.

The surgeon carefully dissects back and forth to define all critical structures, achieving the critical view of safety. The cystic duct and cystic artery are clearly seen entering the gallbladder without intervening structures, and the common bile duct is easily visible.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully place plastic clips across the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring that the common bile duct remains unobstructed during the procedure.

With the plastic clips securely placed on the cystic duct and artery, the surgeon switches to a cautery device to carefully cut across the cystic artery, followed by the cystic duct using a specialized cut setting. The video then speeds up to five times normal speed as the gallbladder is removed from the liver bed.
The surgeon approaches the top of the gallbladder, where the plane between the gallbladder and liver is less distinct, likely due to inflammation from previous gallbladder attacks.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site for any signs of bleeding after the complete removal of the gallbladder. An assistant inserts a retrieval bag through the port in the left upper side, and the gallbladder is placed inside the bag for removal.
I appreciate you taking the time to watch. If you have any questions, please feel free to leave them in the comments.
